reasoning: The FRED data indicates the EFFR was at 3.64 as of Dec 21, 2025. The statistical tool's linear model gives a 0.30 probability of an increase, while the combined model gave 0.48 (skewed by last year's structurally higher rate of 4.33). Because we are firmly in a Fed cutting cycle (recent 25 bps cut down to 3.64, with the CME FedWatch showing no hikes and more cuts priced in for 2026), any increase would strictly stem from a minor intermeeting upward drift, as we saw in Oct-Nov when EFFR drifted up by 2 basis points. Given this, I assign a 0.32 probability for the near-term Dec 28 horizon. Following the strict instruction that uncertainty must increase (pushing probabilities closer to 0.5) for more distant forecasts, I scale the probability up to 0.41 for Jan 20 and 0.48 for March 21.
